zh-tw.title: 服務業心聲語錄
zh-tw.description: 穿上制服那一刻，就把脾氣留在置物櫃。寫給公車司機、店員、外場、客服——所有把眼淚調成微笑模式的人。

en.title: Service Industry Voice Quotes
en.description: The moment the uniform goes on, the temper goes in the locker. For bus drivers, clerks, waitstaff, and call-center agents — anyone who switches their tears to smile mode for a paycheck.

items.sourceNote: Sparked by 2026-04-29 公車司機 incident covered by CNN — the 「我朋友來過比較便宜」 unreasonable-customer trope is a canonical 奧客 format from Dcard 心情/工作 boards

zh-tw.content:
「我朋友上禮拜來這個價錢」
「請問您朋友的名字是？」
「我跟你又不熟。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「我朋友、我同事、我鄰居」開頭的句子轟炸過的服務業朋友。重點不是真的有沒有那位朋友，而是對方知道你查不到。
variations: 客人說的「我朋友」，通常住在「資料庫查無此人」那一區。

en.content:
"My friend got this cheaper last week."
"What's your friend's name?"
"Like you'd know."
en.editorNote: Send this to anyone in service who's been hit with the "my friend / my coworker / my neighbor" opener today. The point isn't whether the friend is real — it's that the customer knows you can't check.
variations: The customer's "friend" usually lives in the part of the database labeled "no record found."

items.sourceNote: Inspired by ETtoday 「服務業崩潰十大時刻」 list; rewritten with iced-latte specific scenario as a fresh angle

zh-tw.content:
「老闆，這杯為什麼是冰的？」
「因為您點的是冰拿鐵。」
「我是說我要……加冰。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 餐飲業每天的真實對話。傳給上班時剛被同樣邏輯攻擊過的同事，附一句「你不是一個人」。
variations: 點冰的問為什麼是冰的，這不是奧客，這是哲學家。

en.content:
"Why is this iced?"
"You ordered an iced latte."
"Yeah, but I want it... iced."
en.editorNote: Real food-service dialogue, daily. Send to a coworker who got hit with the same logic — add "you are not alone."
variations: Ordering iced and asking why it's iced isn't a difficult customer. It's a philosopher.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 104 職場力 「奧客類型」 article; the no-defect-just-doesn't-suit-me return is a canonical retail Dcard story

zh-tw.content:
「我要退這件衣服。」
「請問是哪裡有問題嗎？」
「沒有，我先生說我穿不好看。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 退貨第一名的理由不是瑕疵，是「我家人說的」。傳給做零售的朋友，他這禮拜大概聽過十次。
variations: 客人說「沒事，但要退」的時候，通常是在退一場家庭會議的結果。

en.content:
"I'd like to return this shirt."
"Is there a defect?"
"No. My husband said it doesn't suit me."
en.editorNote: The #1 return reason isn't a defect — it's "someone in my family said so." Send this to a retail friend; they've probably heard it ten times this week.
variations: When a customer says "nothing's wrong, I just want to return it," they're usually returning the verdict of a family meeting.

items.sourceNote: Original metaphor expanding the Dcard 「制服與情緒切換」 motif; locker-as-emotion-storage angle is original phrasing

zh-tw.content:
我穿上制服那一刻，
就把今天的脾氣留在置物櫃裡了。
下班時再把脾氣領回來，順便領一份薪水的零頭。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合下班後 IG 限動 配自己的制服照。重點不是抱怨，是承認制服底下還有一個會生氣的真人。
variations: 上班時把人格寄放在置物櫃，下班再帶回家——只是回家後，那個人格通常已經沒電了。

en.content:
The moment I put on the uniform,
I leave my temper in the locker.
After the shift I pick it up — along with the loose change of a paycheck.
en.editorNote: Pairs well with an IG story of your own uniform after clocking out. The point isn't complaining — it's admitting there's a real person under the uniform who still gets angry.
variations: We check our personalities into a locker at clock-in, pick them up at clock-out — and by then they're usually out of battery.

items.sourceNote: Original — extends 「笑著上班崩潰下班」 trope with the queueing-tears image, a fresh visual

zh-tw.content:
我在櫃檯笑了一整天。
回家才想起，我已經三天沒哭了。
原來眼淚也是要排隊的，跟我們的客人一樣。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合凌晨睡不著時放在限動。不需要解釋，懂的人會私訊你。
variations: 服務業的眼淚不是不流，是排到了才能流。

en.content:
I smiled at the counter all day.
Got home and realized I hadn't cried in three days.
Turns out my tears have to queue too — just like our customers.
en.editorNote: Good for a 2am Insta story when you can't sleep. No explanation needed. The people who get it will DM you.
variations: It's not that service workers don't cry. It's that the tears have to take a number first.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 商周 「服務業7年」 piece on emotional-labor multitasking; original four-action breakdown is a fresh structural framing

zh-tw.content:
服務業教會我最厲害的技能：
客人在罵的時候，
眼睛點頭、嘴巴道歉、
心裡放空、手繼續操作。
四件事互不衝突，這叫情緒勞動。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給沒在服務業工作過、不懂為什麼你回家不想說話的家人朋友。這不是脾氣大，這是技能型疲勞。
variations: 外人以為服務業在出力氣，其實我們在出『四個我』。

en.content:
Service work taught me the ultimate multitask:
nodding with my eyes, apologizing with my mouth,
going blank in my head, still ringing them up.
Four separate jobs at once. This is emotional labor.
en.editorNote: Send this to family or friends who don't get why you go quiet after work. It isn't a bad temper — it's skill-based exhaustion.
variations: People think service work uses your body. It actually uses four versions of you, all at the same time.

items.sourceNote: Subverts the canonical 「顧客就是上帝」 cliche; original reframe of the worship metaphor is the punchline

zh-tw.content:
「顧客就是上帝」的意思是：
您可以信仰我，
但不能拜託我替您處理
您人生中所有不如意的事。
zh-tw.editorNote: 下次有人對你引用這句話的時候，可以微笑回他這個版本。比較適合貼在員工休息室牆上，而不是當客人面唸出來。
variations: 顧客是上帝沒錯，問題是上帝通常不會大聲叫服務生。

en.content:
"The customer is god" means:
you may worship me.
It does not mean I am responsible
for everything that's gone wrong in your life this week.
en.editorNote: Next time someone quotes the original at you, smile and reply with this version. Probably better posted in the break room than read aloud to a customer.
variations: Yes, the customer is god. But god usually doesn't yell at the waiter.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Newtalk 公車司機 op-ed structural critique; the 「最多笑容、最少尊重」 antithesis is a fresh framing

zh-tw.content:
服務業是一個奇怪的地方——
付出最多笑容，得到最少尊重；
說最多「不好意思」，
最不好意思的卻是別人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合配一張你制服的全身照，當作 1024×1024 的限動文字。是一句不用解釋就有共感的話。
variations: 服務業的不公平，不是薪水低，是你笑得最多，卻被當成最該被罵的人。

en.content:
Service work is a strange place:
you offer the most smiles, get the least respect.
You say "sorry" the most,
while the people who should be sorry are everyone else.
en.editorNote: Pairs well with a full-length photo of your uniform as a 1024x1024 story caption. Needs no explanation if you've worked the floor.
variations: The unfairness isn't the wage. It's that you smile the most and somehow end up the one being yelled at.

items.sourceNote: Reframes 「客人是上帝」 with the 隔壁桌 / next-table image — originated from public commentary around the 公車司機 incident

zh-tw.content:
客人不是上帝。
客人是隔壁桌也在等的人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 短到可以當招牌、印在杯墊、貼在外場員工通道。給「我有付錢就比較大」的客人最溫柔的提醒。
variations: 你不是上帝，你只是先進來而已。

en.content:
The customer isn't a god.
The customer is the person at the next table, also waiting.
en.editorNote: Short enough to print on a coaster, a sign, or the staff hallway door. The gentlest reminder for the customer who thinks paying makes them bigger.
variations: You're not god. You just got here first.

items.sourceNote: Direct response framing to 2026-04-29 公車司機事件 — original 「他刷的是車費，不是你的尊嚴」 separation is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
給今天遇到奧客的你：
你不是欠他的。
他刷的是車費，是商品的價錢，
不是你尊嚴的價錢。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天剛被罵完正在後台躲著哭的同事。這句話請收藏，下班後再看一次，明天還可以再看一次。
variations: 他付的錢買的是服務，不是把你當情緒垃圾桶的權利。

en.content:
To anyone who met a difficult customer today:
you don't owe them anything.
They paid the fare, the price tag — not your dignity.
en.editorNote: Send this to the coworker hiding in the back room crying after being yelled at. Save it. Read it again after work. Read it again tomorrow.
variations: They paid for service. Not for the right to use you as an emotional trash can.

items.sourceNote: Original cross-industry solidarity framing; uniform / sorry-weight image is rewritten from the broader Dcard 服務業 register

zh-tw.content:
公車司機、便利商店店員、
餐廳外場、客服專員——
我們穿不同的制服，
但每天說的「不好意思」，
都是同一個重量。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合公車司機事件後一陣子刷到的時候轉發，當作給整個服務業的擁抱。不分行業。
variations: 我們的制服不一樣，但下班後脫下來的疲憊，是同一個顏色。

en.content:
Bus drivers, convenience store clerks,
waitstaff, call-center agents —
different uniforms,
but the "sorry" we say all day
weighs exactly the same.
en.editorNote: Repost as a quiet hug for everyone in service after a viral incident — across industries, not just one.
variations: Different uniforms, same color of exhaustion when we take them off.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by udn 「職場厭世語錄」 wage-vs-prices format; rewritten with vegetable-pricing metaphor specific to service work

zh-tw.content:
公司要我們把每個客人都當貴賓服務，
卻把我們的薪水當白菜在賣。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合月底剛入帳那天看著存款餘額苦笑。員工群組的萬能標題。
variations: 客人是貴賓，員工是耗材，這就是這個產業的奇蹟。

en.content:
The company wants us to treat every customer like royalty,
while pricing our paycheck like cabbage.
en.editorNote: Perfect for staring at your bank balance the day after payday. A universal headline for the staff group chat.
variations: Customers are VIPs, employees are consumables. That's the magic of this industry.

items.sourceNote: Original — pairs the per-hour-wage / per-second-emotional-labor structural framing as a fresh contrast format

zh-tw.content:
八小時站到腿軟，
三十秒就被嫌服務不夠笑——
時薪是按一小時算的，
情緒，卻是按秒在計算的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 輪班結束坐下脫鞋的那一刻最有共感。傳給所有把腳泡熱水才能睡著的同行。
variations: 我們的時間值多少錢，跟我們的情緒值多少錢，公司算的是兩本帳。

en.content:
Eight hours on aching feet.
Thirty seconds for them to complain you didn't smile enough.
The wage is calculated by the hour.
The emotional labor — by the second.
en.editorNote: Hits hardest the moment you sit down and pull your shoes off. Send to anyone who has to soak their feet before bed.
variations: What our time is worth and what our emotions are worth — the company keeps two separate ledgers.

items.sourceNote: Original — the 「制服燙得平整 / 薪水條皺得理直氣壯」 contrast is a fresh visual; pairs polished-presentation tropes against the wrinkled paycheck

zh-tw.content:
制服要燙得平整，
笑容要訓練得標準，
頭髮要綁得整齊——
只有薪水條，皺得最理直氣壯。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合月底拍一張薪水條配這段話。誠實到不需要任何 hashtag。
variations: 公司管你的衣服、笑容、頭髮，唯獨不管你的薪水有沒有跟得上物價。

en.content:
Pressed uniform.
Trained smile.
Hair tied neatly back.
Only the paycheck dares to show up wrinkled.
en.editorNote: Pair with a photo of your wrinkled paycheck on payday. Honest enough to need no hashtag.
variations: The company runs your uniform, your smile, your hair — just not whether your wage keeps up with rent.

items.sourceNote: Original — extends the 「奧客結帳前後判若兩人」 register from Dcard 工作板; sugar-then-vinegar emotional-shift framing is original

zh-tw.content:
客人結帳前：「妹妹妳好可愛，這個多少錢？」
結帳後：「妳剛剛說多少？我聽不懂中文嗎？」
中間只差一張發票的時間。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天剛經歷過「結帳前後變臉秀」的櫃檯同事。重點在那個微笑撐不過十秒的速度。
variations: 客人從『妹妹』叫到『妳』，中間就只刷了一張卡的時間。

en.content:
Before checkout: "You're so sweet, how much is this?"
After checkout: "What did you say? Do I not speak English?"
The gap between them is one printed receipt.
en.editorNote: Send to the cashier coworker who just survived a Jekyll-and-Hyde transition today. The whole joke is how fast that smile drops.
variations: It takes about ten seconds for "sweetie" to become "hey you," and one swipe of a card.

items.sourceNote: Original — closing-time customer trope rewritten with the door-locked-but-eyes-open visual; standard PTT 打工板 register

zh-tw.content:
打烊前十分鐘，門才剛要鎖，
客人從外面探頭：「還有開嗎？」
我笑著點頭。
眼睛裡，已經把椅子翻上桌了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 餐飲業夜班的標準收尾。傳給最近一週連續被「最後一個客人」拖到加班的朋友。
variations: 服務業的『歡迎光臨』，最後十分鐘是用一條命換的。

en.content:
Ten minutes to close. I'm reaching for the lock.
A head pops in: "Still open?"
I smile and nod.
Behind my eyes, I've already flipped every chair onto the tables.
en.editorNote: The classic late-shift closer for food service. Send this to anyone who got stuck past clock-out three times this week by "just one more customer."
variations: The last ten minutes of a service shift are paid for in life force, not wages.

items.sourceNote: Original — call-center 「靜音鍵就是逃生口」 metaphor; rewritten from the broader 客服日常 register on 104 部落格

zh-tw.content:
做客服三年，最熟練的不是話術，
是按下靜音鍵的時機——
剛好讓對方聽不到我的嘆氣，
卻又不會錯過他罵到一半的暫停。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給戴著耳機在公司隔板裡求生的客服朋友。靜音鍵不是功能，是逃生口。
variations: 客服的專業不是聲音甜，是知道什麼時候把麥克風關掉、再打開。

en.content:
Three years on the phones. My sharpest skill isn't the script —
it's the exact timing of the mute button:
late enough to hide my sigh,
early enough not to miss the pause in his shouting.
en.editorNote: Send to the headset-wearing call-center friend surviving inside a cubicle. The mute button isn't a feature — it's a fire escape.
variations: Call-center expertise isn't a sweet voice. It's knowing exactly when to kill the mic and when to bring it back.

items.sourceNote: Original reframe of the 「我們也有家人」 service-worker plea; daughter-mother-son three-line image is a fresh structural framing

zh-tw.content:
罵我的時候請記得：
我是某個爸爸的女兒，
是某個小孩的媽媽，
是某個老人家在家裡等的兒子。
制服底下，我們不只是一個工號。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合公司內部訓練教材或分店牆面標語。也適合轉發給最近對服務人員失去耐心的長輩朋友——溫柔的提醒。
variations: 你罵的不是一個職稱，是一個家裡有人在等他下班的人。

en.content:
Before you yell, remember:
I'm somebody's daughter,
somebody's mom,
somebody's son whose elderly parent is waiting up tonight.
Under the uniform, we are not just an employee number.
en.editorNote: Works on internal training materials or as a wall poster in the back office. Also good to forward to the older relative who's lost patience with service staff lately — a gentle reminder.
variations: You're not yelling at a job title. You're yelling at someone whose family is waiting for them to come home.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 換日線 「有限消費 vs 無限服務」 observation; original 一杯50元 metaphor for unlimited-service expectation is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人花了五十塊，
以為買到的是這杯飲料、
那張收據、
還有我整個人生的耐心。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「我有付錢欸」開頭轟炸過的同行。錢買的是商品，不是員工的整副人格。
variations: 五十塊買到的是飲料，不是把店員當吵架對象的會員資格。

en.content:
They paid two bucks for a drink
and thought it came with the receipt,
the straw,
and the rest of my life's patience.
en.editorNote: Send this to anyone hit with the "but I paid you" opener this week. Money buys the product, not the employee's entire personality.
variations: Two bucks bought you a drink. It didn't buy you membership in a club where you get to yell at the clerk.

items.sourceNote: Original — extends the Dcard 「服務至上」 critique with a fresh 「服務業 ≠ 服侍業」 wordplay; one-character distinction is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
我做的是服務業，不是服侍業。
差一個字，差的是我有沒有跪下來的義務。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合做成員工休息室的小卡，或印在識別證背面。一個字的差別，整個人格的距離。
variations: 服務跟服侍只差一個字，但中間隔的是一整條尊嚴的線。

en.content:
I work in service — not in servitude.
One letter different. One whole question of whether I owe you my knees.
en.editorNote: Print it on a break-room card or stick it on the back of your name badge. One letter, one entire boundary.
variations: Service and servitude — one letter apart, a whole dignity apart.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Susan David's emotional-labor 「professional game face」 framing; original 「微笑是我的制服第二件」 layered-uniform image is the rewrite

zh-tw.content:
我們的制服有兩件：
一件是公司發的，
一件是微笑——
後面那件，沒有人幫你洗，
也沒有人替你燙。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天回家把笑容跟制服一起脫下來、卻發現微笑黏在臉上拿不下來的同行。
variations: 微笑是員工自費的第二套制服，髒了還要自己處理。

en.content:
We wear two uniforms:
the one the company hands out,
and the smile.
Nobody washes the second one for you.
Nobody irons it either.
en.editorNote: Send this to anyone who peels off both the uniform and the smile after a shift — and notices the smile sometimes won't come off.
variations: The smile is uniform number two. Bought by you, dry-cleaned by you, and never reimbursed.

items.sourceNote: Original — extends the 方格子 「客人欺善怕惡」 observation with a fresh manager-summoning trope; the 「店長出來他就變人」 pivot is the rewrite

zh-tw.content:
客人對我大小聲整整十分鐘。
店長出來，他突然會用敬語了。
原來他不是聽不懂禮貌，
是分得清誰罵得起、誰罵不起。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被客人挑軟柿子吃、店長一出現就被當隱形人的同事。看得最清楚的，永遠是被欺負的那個。
variations: 客人的禮貌，是看職稱發放的，不是看人發放的。

en.content:
He yelled at me for ten minutes straight.
Then the manager walked over and suddenly he remembered his manners.
Turns out he understood politeness all along —
he just knew exactly who he could afford to yell at.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who got picked on today and went invisible the moment the manager appeared. The person being bullied always sees it the clearest.
variations: Customer politeness is distributed by job title, not by humanity.

items.sourceNote: Reframes PTT WomenTalk 「服務業的禮貌只是防客訴」 thread; rewritten as a defensive-armor metaphor instead of the original complaint frame

zh-tw.content:
我對你笑，不是因為喜歡你。
是因為公司教我，
微笑是這份工作裡最便宜的盔甲——
穿上它，才不會在客訴信裡被點名。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合給以為服務人員的笑容是真心的客人看一眼。微笑不是情感表達，是職業安全裝備。
variations: 服務業的微笑是防客訴用的，跟工地的安全帽一樣，只是大家看不見。

en.content:
I'm not smiling because I like you.
The company taught me
that a smile is the cheapest armor this job hands out —
put it on, or get named in the complaint email.
en.editorNote: Show this to anyone who thinks service smiles are genuine. The smile isn't a feeling — it's safety gear.
variations: Service-industry smiles are PPE against customer complaints. Same as a hard hat — you just can't see it.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 商周 餐飲業十個現實 piece on the 夾心餅乾 dynamic; original 「老闆要我笑、客人要我跪、我自己要我活下去」 three-pull framing is the rewrite

zh-tw.content:
老闆要我笑得像招牌，
客人要我跪得像道歉，
我自己只想要我下班還能像個人。
三個人拉同一條繩子，斷掉的永遠是中間那段。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被夾在老闆 KPI 跟客人情緒中間、回家連話都不想說的同行。中間那段叫做員工，不叫做緩衝墊。
variations: 老闆、客人、員工三方拉鋸，斷的不會是繩子，是中間的人。

en.content:
The boss wants me smiling like a logo.
The customer wants me kneeling like an apology.
I just want to clock out still recognizable as a person.
Three people pulling on one rope — the part that snaps is always the middle.
en.editorNote: Send this to anyone caught between a boss's KPIs and a customer's mood, going home too tired to talk. The middle of that rope is called an employee, not a shock absorber.
variations: Three-way tug-of-war. The rope doesn't break. The person in the middle does.

items.sourceNote: Original — extends the Threads 便利商店超難 register with a fresh job-description-as-novel framing; the 「徵店員」 vs 「徵超人」 contrast is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
徵人公告寫著：徵店員，月薪 28K。
實際工作內容：收銀、補貨、煮咖啡、蒸包子、
收包裹、繳水電費、處理客訴、清廁所、
還要笑得像在拍廣告。
28K 請的不是店員，是超人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合配一張徵才海報的照片。傳給最近在便利商店打工的學弟妹，告訴他們撐不下去不是你的錯。
variations: 便利商店的職缺欄寫『店員』，工作內容欄要分頁才寫得完。

en.content:
Job listing: Clerk wanted, $1,000 a month.
Actual duties: cashier, restock, brew coffee, steam buns,
receive parcels, process bill payments, handle complaints, clean the bathroom,
and smile like you're filming a commercial.
That wage doesn't hire a clerk. It hires a superhero.
en.editorNote: Pair with a photo of a help-wanted poster. Send to the younger cousin pulling shifts at a convenience store and remind them that quitting isn't failure.
variations: The job title says "clerk." The actual duty list needs a second page.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Cheers 微笑是真心 vs 是工作 debate; original 「真心是有保存期限的」 perishable-emotion framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人問：「妳的笑容是真心的嗎？」
我說：「上午是。」
他問：「下午呢？」
我說：「下午是專業的。
真心也有保存期限，過了就只能冷藏販售。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給下午班開始就掛著職業微笑的同行。真心不是無限的，是會耗盡的——這不是冷漠，是物理。
variations: 服務業的真心，每天早上現做，過了中午只能加熱再賣。

en.content:
Customer: "Is that smile real?"
Me: "In the morning, yes."
Customer: "And in the afternoon?"
Me: "In the afternoon it's professional.
Sincerity has a shelf life. After that we can only sell it refrigerated."
en.editorNote: Send to anyone whose smile shifts to autopilot the moment afternoon shift starts. Sincerity isn't infinite — it depletes. That's not coldness, it's physics.
variations: Service-industry sincerity is made fresh daily. After noon, it's reheated.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the ETtoday 凱特王 「服務業心底冒出的一句話」 column; original 「我之前都可以」 customer-line as deja-vu format is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人說：「我之前都可以，為什麼現在不行？」
我心裡想：「您的『之前』，跟我們的規則之間，
隔的可能是兩任店長、三次系統升級、
以及您不願意再說一次的那段歷史。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天都要解釋為什麼規則改了的櫃檯同事。「我之前」是奧客最愛的時光機，可惜車票永遠在他自己口袋裡。
variations: 客人的「之前」是平行宇宙，跟我們公司的規則沒有時間軸上的交集。

en.content:
Customer: "It worked for me before. Why can't I do it now?"
Me, internally: "Your 'before' is two managers ago,
three system updates back,
and a chapter of history you'd rather not retell."
en.editorNote: Send this to the front-counter coworker who explains policy changes ten times a day. "Before" is the customer's favorite time machine — and the ticket is always in their own pocket.
variations: The customer's "before" is a parallel universe. It doesn't share a timeline with our actual policy.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Cottonwood Psychology 2026 retail-worker reflection on small drains; the 「一句話 = 一格電池」 phone-battery metaphor is original to this rewrite

zh-tw.content:
下班的我，像一支只剩 3% 電的手機。
白天每一句「不好意思」都是一格電，
每一個強迫的微笑都是後台跑著的 app，
回家連回訊息的力氣，都得從充電器借。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合凌晨回家躺著滑手機時的限動配文。情緒勞動的疲憊不是你脆弱，是電池真的耗光了。
variations: 服務業下班的疲憊，是手機級的低電量警告，不是個性問題。

en.content:
Off-shift me is a phone at 3% battery.
Every "sorry" today drained a bar.
Every forced smile was an app running in the background.
By bedtime, even replying to a text has to be borrowed from the charger.
en.editorNote: Pairs well with a late-night scrolling-in-bed Insta story. The exhaustion isn't weakness — the battery is actually empty.
variations: Service-industry tired is a low-battery warning, not a personality flaw.

items.sourceNote: Original — extends the Dcard 「奧客經典語錄」 thread with a fresh 「別家店都可以」 retort framing; the 「別家店」 = 平行宇宙 reframe is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人最愛的句型：「別家店都可以，為什麼你們不行？」
標準答案：「那您去別家。」
但我們不能講，所以只能笑著說：「不好意思，公司規定。」
這句『不好意思』，不是道歉，是替公司付的封口費。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天都要重複「公司規定」這四個字、講到自己都不相信的同行。我們的禮貌，公司用得最開心。
variations: 「別家店都可以」是客人最愛的咒語，咒到的不是別家店，是站在你眼前這個店員。

en.content:
Customer's favorite line: "Every other store does it. Why can't you?"
The honest answer: "Then go there."
The one we can say: "I'm sorry, that's company policy."
That "sorry" isn't an apology. It's hush money the company makes us pay out of our own mouths.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who repeats "company policy" so many times a day they've stopped believing it. Our politeness — the company gets the most use out of it.
variations: "Every other store does it" is the customer's favorite spell. The one it hits isn't the other store — it's the clerk standing right in front of them.

items.sourceNote: Original — extends the 巴哈職場板 thread on coworker survival with a fresh 「下班前最後一個客人」 lottery-ticket framing

zh-tw.content:
服務業最公平的事情：
下班前最後一個客人是天使還是魔鬼，
完全看運氣——
跟刮刮樂一樣，
你只能祈禱，不能挑。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合輪班最後二十分鐘默念的禱告詞。傳給今天剛中『下班奧客大獎』的同事，附一句「我懂」。
variations: 下班前最後一個客人，是服務業的隨機事件，沒有 SOP 能擋。

en.content:
The fairest thing about service work:
whether your last customer of the shift is an angel or a demon
is pure luck —
like a scratch ticket.
You pray. You don't pick.
en.editorNote: A good silent prayer for the final twenty minutes of a shift. Send to the coworker who just hit the "clock-out customer from hell" jackpot — add "I get it."
variations: The last customer of a shift is a random event. No SOP blocks it.

items.sourceNote: Reframes Laura E Fox Medium piece on hidden emotional-labor cost; original 「沒寫進職缺」 invisible-job-description angle is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
職缺寫的：負責結帳、補貨、簡單清潔。
沒寫的：替客人的爛心情墊底、
替老闆的 KPI 演戲、
替自己的眼淚找一個沒人看見的角落。
薪水算的是有寫的，沒寫的，是免費贈送。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近覺得自己「明明只是站著，怎麼這麼累」的同行。累的不是工作本身，是那些沒寫進職缺裡的部分。
variations: 服務業的薪水算明的，情緒勞動算暗的，公司收的是套餐，員工付的是加大。

en.content:
What the job listing said: cashier, restocking, light cleaning.
What it didn't say: cushion the customer's bad mood,
perform for the boss's KPIs,
find a corner where nobody can see you cry.
The wage covers what was written. The rest comes free.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who can't figure out why "just standing around" is so exhausting. It isn't the job — it's the parts that never made it into the listing.
variations: The wage covers the visible job. Emotional labor is the upgrade the company eats and the employee pays for.

items.sourceNote: Reframes Dcard 心情板 「服務業憑什麼要人家一直體諒你？」 thread; flips the rhetorical question back as a mirror — original framing of customer self-question

zh-tw.content:
客人問：「你們服務業憑什麼要我體諒？」
我說：「沒有要您體諒，
只是想拜託您，
把對待您家人的那一成基本禮貌，
借給我用八小時就好。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合貼在門口的客人須知旁邊。我們不是要被同情，只是希望客人把家裡的禮貌帶一點點到店裡。
variations: 服務業沒有要您憐憫，只是希望您講話的時候，記得對面也是人。

en.content:
Customer: "Why should I have to be considerate to service workers?"
Me: "We're not asking for sympathy.
We're asking you to lend us
ten percent of the manners you use at home —
just for the eight hours of our shift."
en.editorNote: Pin this near the customer notice at the door. We aren't asking to be pitied — just for a little of the politeness people use at home to come into the store.
variations: Service workers aren't begging for pity. We're asking you to remember the person across the counter is also human.

items.sourceNote: Reframes Modern Health 2026 retail-workforce mental-health stat (67% burnout); original 「三個同事就有兩個在燒」 wildfire-metaphor framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
報告說：每三個服務業同事，就有兩個在燒。
我們不是在工作，是在輪流當柴火。
燒完的人離職，沒燒完的人補位，
公司管這叫——人力資源週轉率。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近想離職、又怕被說『抗壓性低』的同行。你不是抗壓性低，你是燒到下一根了。
variations: 服務業沒有人力短缺，只有不夠快補進來的下一根柴火。

en.content:
The report says: two out of three service workers are burning out.
We're not working. We're taking turns as kindling.
The ones who burn through quit. The unburnt ones cover.
The company calls this — workforce turnover.
en.editorNote: Send this to anyone considering quitting who's afraid they'll be called "low resilience." It isn't low resilience. The previous log already burned through.
variations: Service work doesn't have a labor shortage. It has a problem refilling the next log fast enough.

items.sourceNote: Original — extends the Dcard 「客人總是認為自己是對的」 thread with a fresh courtroom-verdict framing; the 「客人是法官也是被告」 dual-role image is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
在客訴的法庭上，
客人是原告、是法官，順便也是陪審團。
我們是被告，沒有律師，
判決書還要自己念出來——
「不好意思，是我的疏忽。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天剛在客訴信上簽完名、自己唸自己罪狀的同事。這場審判沒有上訴庭，只有下一個客人。
variations: 服務業的客訴流程，是客人寫劇本，員工演自己被罰錢的那一段。

en.content:
In the courtroom of customer complaints,
the customer is plaintiff, judge, and jury.
We're the defendant. No lawyer.
We also have to read the verdict ourselves —
"I'm sorry, that was my oversight."
en.editorNote: Send this to the coworker who just signed off on a complaint letter today, reading their own charges aloud. There's no appeals court — just the next customer.
variations: Customer-complaint procedure: the customer writes the script, the employee performs the part where they get fined.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Retail Tech Innovation Hub 排班-不穩 burnout angle; original 「排班是抽籤、休假是奢侈品」 lottery-metaphor framing is the rewrite

zh-tw.content:
服務業的人生規劃：
排班表出來才知道下個月能不能去婚禮，
休假是抽籤抽到的，
生病要看公司今天缺不缺人。
別人在計畫人生，我們在等公告。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每個月十五號緊盯班表、等著看自己人生能不能繼續的同行。我們的時間不是自己的，是公司還沒派出去的那部分。
variations: 別人有行事曆，服務業有班表——一個是規劃，一個是判決。

en.content:
Service-industry life planning:
the schedule drops, then I know if I can attend the wedding next month.
Days off come by lottery.
Sick leave depends on whether the store is short-staffed today.
Others plan their lives. We wait for the schedule.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone refreshing the schedule app on the 15th every month, waiting to see if their life can continue. Our time isn't ours — it's the part the company hasn't assigned yet.
variations: Other people have calendars. Service workers have schedules — one is a plan, the other is a verdict.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Dcard 「現在服務業態度是不是越來越差」 thread; original 「態度差不是退化，是電池沒換」 battery-not-replaced framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人說：「現在服務業態度怎麼越來越差？」
不是態度差，是電池沒換。
您看到的不耐煩，
是公司省下的人力、薪水、休假，
累積在員工臉上的利息。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每個被嫌「服務態度不好」、其實只是已經連上十二天班的同事。態度不是個性，是被壓榨後剩下的形狀。
variations: 服務態度差，不是員工變壞，是公司沒幫員工換電池。

en.content:
Customer: "Why is service so much worse these days?"
It's not bad service. The battery hasn't been changed.
The impatience you see
is the headcount, the wages, the days off the company saved —
compounding as interest on the employee's face.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone accused of "bad attitude" who's actually just on day 12 of a 12-day stretch. Attitude isn't personality — it's the shape left behind after the squeeze.
variations: Service isn't getting worse. The company just isn't replacing the batteries.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Dcard 「養奧客」 thread on management-fed entitlement; original 「公司養出來的奧客，最後是員工餵的」 food-chain framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人不是天生奧的，
是公司一次又一次「客人最大」教出來的——
第一次退貨破例，第二次發誓再也不破例，
第三次客人寄信給總公司，破例變成 SOP。
奧客是公司養的，但最後吃到苦的，是站在最前面那個。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天替公司「破例政策」道歉的同事。我們不是在處理客人，是在替老闆收他自己埋的單。
variations: 公司怕一封客訴信，員工就要替整套破例制度站第一線。

en.content:
Difficult customers aren't born — they're trained,
by a company that keeps saying "customer is king."
First exception: just this once. Second exception: never again.
Third exception: an email reaches HQ, and "never again" becomes the new SOP.
The company raises them. The frontline pays the bill.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone apologizing daily for the company's "just this once" exceptions. We aren't handling customers — we're paying off the tab the boss left open.
variations: Corporate fears one complaint email. Employees pay for the policy that fear writes.

items.sourceNote: Reframes Mental Health First Aid 2026 retail-stress piece; original 「下班三十分鐘解鎖人格」 reentry-window framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
我下班後需要三十分鐘，
誰都不要跟我講話。
那不是冷漠，是解鎖——
要把『客服模式』下載出來，
才能裝回『我自己』那個版本。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合貼在房門外，給家人朋友看。下班後的安靜不是不愛你，是系統正在切換使用者。
variations: 服務業下班後的沉默，是回家路上正在更新韌體，不是在生氣。

en.content:
Give me thirty minutes after I clock out.
Don't talk to me. Not yet.
That isn't coldness. It's unlocking —
uninstalling the customer-service build
so the "actually-me" version can boot up again.
en.editorNote: Post this on the door for family and roommates. The post-shift silence isn't rejection — the system is switching users.
variations: Post-shift silence is firmware updating on the way home, not anger.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the ETtoday 凱特王 「服務業心底話」 column with a fresh 「您不是顧客，您是路人」 customer-vs-passerby distinction; the demotion-by-behavior framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
顧客是顧客，路人是路人。
進門有禮貌、結帳會說謝謝的，
我們叫您顧客。
進門大小聲、結帳像在審犯人的，
那叫路人——
剛好經過、剛好花了錢、剛好不被歡迎再來。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合貼在收銀機旁邊小聲提醒自己。顧客身分不是付錢就有的，是用態度換的。
variations: 錢買的是商品，不是『顧客』這個身分；身分要您自己用禮貌換。

en.content:
A customer is a customer. A passerby is a passerby.
Walk in with manners, leave with a thank-you — that's a customer.
Walk in shouting, treat checkout like an interrogation — that's a passerby.
Someone who happened to come by,
happened to spend money,
and happens not to be welcomed back.
en.editorNote: Tape this near the register as a quiet reminder to yourself. Customer status isn't bought at checkout — it's earned with manners.
variations: Money buys the product. The title "customer" — that's earned with how you talk.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Cottonwood Psychology 2026 retail-reflection on cumulative micro-drains; original 「一天一千個小傷口」 paper-cut metaphor is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
服務業不會被一刀砍倒，
是被一千個小傷口磨垮的——
一句翻白眼、一聲嘖、一個沒說謝謝的轉身、
一張甩在櫃檯上的卡。
每一個都不夠告人，加起來剛好夠把一個人耗光。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近覺得「沒被罵但就是好累」的同行。沒有大事，才是這個工作最累的地方——一千個小傷口加起來，比一刀還深。
variations: 壓垮服務業員工的，從來不是大事，是上千個沒被算進考勤表的小事。

en.content:
Nobody in service goes down from one big blow.
It's a thousand small cuts —
an eye roll, a tsk, a turn without a thank-you,
a card slapped onto the counter.
None of them big enough to report. All of them, together, just enough to empty a person out.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who says "nobody yelled at me, but I'm wrecked." The exhausting part of this job is the absence of one big thing — a thousand small cuts go deeper than one stab.
variations: Service workers aren't broken by one big incident. We're broken by a thousand small things the timecard never counted.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the udn 「奧客比例最高職業」 piece; original 「客人付的錢買得到杯子，買不到我說『欸』的權利」 transactional-boundary framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人對我彈手指、揮手、拍桌子，
喊一聲「欸」叫我過去。
他付的錢買得到這杯飲料、這份餐、這個位子，
買不到把我從名字降級成「欸」的權利。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被當成「欸」叫過去的同事。我們有名字、有工號、有人生——就是沒有便宜到變成一個語助詞。
variations: 「欸」是叫狗的、不是叫人的；服務業的耐心不是用消費金額換的。

en.content:
He snapped his fingers, waved, banged the table,
yelled "hey" to summon me.
The money he paid bought the drink, the meal, the seat —
not the right to demote me from a name to "hey."
en.editorNote: Send this to the coworker who got "hey-ed" across the floor today. We have names, employee numbers, lives — none of them cheap enough to be reduced to an interjection.
variations: "Hey" is for calling a dog. Service patience isn't sold by the price of the receipt.

items.sourceNote: Subverts the Maya Angelou 「people will never forget how you made them feel」 inspirational quote canon; original employee-side flip 「我們也記得你怎麼讓我們感覺」 mirror framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人會忘記我們說過什麼、做過什麼，
但會記得我們讓他們有什麼感覺。
——順帶一提，
我們也會記得你讓我們有什麼感覺。
只是我們的紀錄沒有 Google 評論可以打。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合貼在員工休息室的牆上，給每天被「客人感受」當成 KPI 的同事。我們的感受沒被打分，但全寫在班表後面那個願不願意再排你那一桌的眼神裡。
variations: 服務業是雙向記憶：客人記得我們的態度，我們也記得客人的眼神。差別只是我們不能寫評論。

en.content:
Customers forget what we said, forget what we did,
but remember how we made them feel.
For the record —
we also remember how you made us feel.
We just don't have a Google review form for it.
en.editorNote: Tape this in the break room for any coworker whose KPI is "customer feeling." Our feelings aren't scored — they live in the look on a server's face when they decide whether to take your table again.
variations: Service is two-way memory. Customers remember our attitude. We remember their eyes. The difference is we don't get to leave a review.

items.sourceNote: Original — extends the Dcard 奧客日常 register with a fresh 「拍照打卡卻嫌等太久」 contradiction format; the menu-as-photo-prop reframe is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人來店裡，先把菜單擺好拍照、
再把飲料擺好拍照、
餐點上來再從三個角度拍照——
然後抱怨：「為什麼上菜這麼慢？」
抱歉，我們沒有「攝影棚速度」這個選項。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被 IG 客人拖到出餐速度被客訴的同事。我們是餐廳，不是 studio——沒辦法替您的限動加班。
variations: 客人先把店當攝影棚，再嫌餐廳出餐慢；矛盾的不是廚房，是客人自己。

en.content:
Customer arrives, arranges the menu for a photo,
styles the drink for a photo,
shoots the entrée from three different angles —
then complains: "Why is the food so slow?"
Sorry, "photoshoot speed" isn't on the menu.
en.editorNote: Send this to the coworker whose ticket time got dinged because the IG-table held up the pass. We run a restaurant, not a studio — we can't pull overtime for your story.
variations: First they treat the place like a studio, then they blame the kitchen for being slow. The contradiction isn't in the back of house.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Cottonwood Psychology 2026 retail-reflection piece on customer micro-choices; original 「謝謝兩個字 = 一杯能量飲料」 fuel-economy framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人結帳時多說那一句「謝謝」，
對你只是兩個字、零點五秒。
對我來說——
是一杯免費的能量飲料、
是下一個小時還能保持微笑的燃料、
是今天回家路上願意對家人多說一句話的力氣。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近覺得「為什麼一句謝謝這麼難」的同行；也適合分享給願意聽的客人朋友——你那兩個字，真的有人在數。
variations: 你給的「謝謝」是零點五秒，我們收到的是一整個下午的續命。

en.content:
When you say "thank you" at checkout,
for you it's two words, half a second.
For me it's a free energy drink,
fuel for one more hour of smiling,
and the strength to say one extra sentence to my family on the way home.
en.editorNote: Send this to anyone wondering why "thanks" feels so rare. Also worth sharing with the customer-side friend who'll listen — those two words are being counted.
variations: Your "thank you" costs half a second. On our end, it pays for the rest of the afternoon.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the TwistedSifter 2026-04 「打烊前 5 分鐘衝進來」 viral retail story; original 「打烊時間是給員工下班的，不是給您加班的」 boundary-reframe is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
「你們不是還沒關門嗎？」
「我們九點打烊，現在 8:55。」
「那還有五分鐘啊。」
那五分鐘是留給我們關燈、關機、結帳、回家——
不是留給您悠閒地逛半小時。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「還有 X 分鐘」綁架的同事。打烊時間是給員工下班用的，不是給客人加班用的。
variations: 打烊前五分鐘進來的客人，通常離開的時間是打烊後三十分鐘。

en.content:
"You're not closed yet, right?"
"We close at 9. It's 8:55."
"So there's still five minutes."
Those five minutes are for us to shut down the registers, turn off the lights, and go home —
not for you to leisurely browse for half an hour.
en.editorNote: Send this to anyone who got hijacked by the "but you have X minutes left" line tonight. Closing time is for the staff to clock out — not for customers to clock in.
variations: The customer who walks in five minutes before close usually walks out thirty minutes after it.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the TwistedSifter 2026-04 「客人嫌員工沒微笑」 viral story; cross-pollinates with 獨立評論 「職業性假笑像思覺失調」 angle — original 「微笑不在我的職務說明書」 contract reframe is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人：「你為什麼不笑？」
我：「您點的是咖啡，還是我的表情？」
微笑不在這杯飲料的價目表裡，
也不在我的職務說明書上——
那是您額外想要的服務，請憑禮貌兌換。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被「為什麼不笑」三個字打斷情緒的同事。微笑是禮物、不是基本配備；您付的錢只買得到飲料，不包含我臉部表情管理。
variations: 微笑要用禮貌換，不是用金錢買；您點的菜單裡沒有「員工表情」這個品項。

en.content:
Customer: "Why aren't you smiling?"
Me: "Did you order a coffee, or my face?"
A smile isn't on this drink's price list,
and it's not in my job description —
it's a service you have to pay for in manners, not dollars.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone whose mood got derailed by "why aren't you smiling?" today. A smile is a gift, not a standard feature. Your receipt covers the coffee — not my facial muscles.
variations: Smiles are paid for in politeness, not currency. "Employee expression" isn't a menu item.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the TwistedSifter 2026-05 「五分錢糾紛打去 HR」 viral story; original 「您為了五塊錢花掉的時薪，比五塊錢貴多了」 cost-benefit reframe is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人為了少找五塊錢，
打電話投訴、要求見店長、
寫信給總公司、留下一星評論、
還威脅要叫我滾蛋。
您今天為了五塊錢花掉的兩個小時，
換算成您的時薪——
已經比那五塊錢貴上一百倍。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「小事大鬧」型客人耗到懷疑人生的同事。客人為五塊錢開的戰，輸的從來不是店家，是他自己的下午。
variations: 為五塊錢鬧兩小時的客人，輸的不是五塊錢，是他整個下午。

en.content:
She fought for five cents short on the change —
called to complain, demanded the manager,
emailed corporate, left a one-star review,
and threatened to get me fired.
The two hours you spent today over five cents,
at your hourly rate —
is already a hundred times more expensive than the five cents.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone exhausted by a "small issue, huge production" customer. The customer who goes to war over a nickel never loses the nickel — they lose their afternoon.
variations: Two hours of rage for five cents in change. They didn't lose the nickel — they lost the afternoon.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the TwistedSifter 2026-05 「叫我加開收銀台」 viral story; original 「您要的不是一個收銀員，是一台複製機」 manpower-reality reframe is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人：「為什麼不多開一台收銀？」
我：「因為今天就我一個人。」
客人：「那叫經理出來啊。」
我：「經理今天請假，由我代理。」
您要的不是「多一個收銀員」，
是「多一個我」——
那不是排班表能解決的，那叫做複製人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「為什麼不多開一台」當靶子的同事。我們是員工，不是分裂出來的影分身；人手不夠不是我們的錯，是排班的人沒到場。
variations: 「為什麼不多開一台」這個問題，問的是收銀台，但答案永遠不在收銀員身上。

en.content:
Customer: "Why don't you open another register?"
Me: "Because I'm the only one here."
Customer: "Then get the manager."
Me: "Manager's off today. I'm covering."
You don't want "another cashier" —
you want "another me."
That's not a scheduling problem. That's cloning.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who got blamed for the line today. We're employees, not shadow clones. "Not enough staff" isn't a problem the people behind the register created.
variations: "Why don't you open another till" is a question about registers, but the answer never lives at the register.

items.sourceNote: Reframes TwistedSifter 2026-05 checkout-operator viral complaint story; the 「客訴你的表情」 angle is a recurring Dcard 服務業 trope rewritten with the smile-as-armor metaphor

zh-tw.content:
客人寫的客訴：
「店員從頭到尾沒有微笑。」
那天我剛從廁所裡哭完出來，
媽媽在加護病房，
手機鈴聲沒停過，
我還是把您要的兩條土司、一瓶牛奶刷完。
您要的是商品，
還是我整張臉？
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「沒笑」客訴的同事。我們刷的是條碼，不是表情；微笑是禮貌，不是您買商品附贈的義務勞動。
variations: 客人客訴你沒笑，是因為他買的不只是商品，他覺得連你的表情都應該打折給他。

en.content:
Customer complaint:
"The cashier never smiled."
That day I'd just come out of the bathroom crying,
my mom was in the ICU,
my phone wouldn't stop buzzing,
and I still rang up your two loaves and a carton of milk.
You bought the groceries.
Did you also buy my face?
en.editorNote: Send this to a coworker who got complained-about for "not smiling." We scan barcodes, not facial expressions. A smile is a courtesy, not an unpaid item bundled with your purchase.
variations: When a customer files a complaint that you didn't smile, what they really wanted to buy on discount was your face.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by TwistedSifter 2026-03 silent-staring-customer viral piece; the 「眼神點餐」 reframe is original commentary on the unspoken-demand customer archetype

zh-tw.content:
客人站在櫃台前不講話，
只是看著我。
我說：「您好，需要什麼嗎？」
他繼續看著我。
我又說：「請問要點餐嗎？」
他嘆了一口氣：
「你怎麼不知道我要什麼？」
先生，我們是服務業，
不是通靈系畢業。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「眼神點餐」過的同事。客人覺得開口是輸，員工要猜是贏；但這個遊戲我們沒報名，獎金也沒人發。
variations: 客人用眼神點餐，員工用眼神算薪水，最後兩個都白忙。

en.content:
Customer walks up. Doesn't speak.
Just stares.
Me: "Hi, how can I help?"
Still staring.
Me: "Are you ready to order?"
He sighs: "How do you not know what I want?"
Sir, this is a service counter,
not a psychic hotline.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who got "ordered by eye contact" today. The customer thinks speaking is losing and guessing is winning — but nobody signed us up for that game, and nobody's paying out the prize.
variations: Customers order with their eyes; employees clock out with theirs. Nobody wins, and the register still has to balance.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Dcard 工作板 「服務業真的只能忍氣吞聲嗎？」 thread; the 「店家賣的是商品，客人買的是出氣筒」 reframe is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人對我大吼，
說我「態度差」。
我整天笑著對五十個人說早安、
幫七個人找零、
替三個人擦掉打翻的咖啡——
結果第五十一個人說我態度差，
公司就相信他。
原來服務業的態度，
是用最後一個遇到的客人來打分。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被一句「態度差」抹掉一整天努力的同事。一整天的笑容不算分，最後一秒的冷臉算全部，這不是服務業的問題，是公司的演算法壞了。
variations: 服務業的考績是最後一個客人寫的，前面五十個微笑都不算。

en.content:
A customer yells at me,
says I have "a bad attitude."
I smiled at fifty people today,
counted change for seven,
wiped up coffee for three —
then the fifty-first one says "bad attitude,"
and the company believes him.
Apparently your service-industry rating
is whatever the last customer of the day wrote.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone whose whole day got erased by one "bad attitude" review. Fifty smiles don't count — the last cold face counts for everything. That's not a service problem; that's a broken algorithm at the top.
variations: Your performance review in service work is written by the last customer you saw. The first fifty smiles don't make the file.

items.sourceNote: Reframes Pleated-Jeans 2026-05-11 round-up of 「the customer isn't always right」 service horror tweets; the 『客人永遠是對的』historical-context reframe is original

zh-tw.content:
「客人永遠是對的」這句話，
是一百年前一個百貨公司老闆說的，
他指的是「不要跟客人吵架，公司會處理」。
一百年後變成：
客人永遠是對的，
所以員工永遠是錯的，
所以公司不用處理，
所以你回家自己消化。
這句話被偷了，連道歉都沒一句。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每一個被「客人永遠是對的」這句話壓過的同事。原句的主詞是公司，不是員工；這一百年下來，主詞被偷偷換了，沒人賠。
variations: 「客人永遠是對的」原意是公司要扛，後來變成員工要忍——同一句話，主詞換了，受傷的人也換了。

en.content:
"The customer is always right" —
a department store owner said it a hundred years ago,
and he meant: "Don't argue with the guest. The company will handle it."
A hundred years later it became:
the customer is always right,
so the employee is always wrong,
so the company handles nothing,
so you go home and swallow it alone.
That sentence got stolen, and nobody apologized.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone crushed under "the customer is always right." The subject of the original sentence was the company, not the employee. Somewhere in the last century the subject got swapped, and nobody paid damages.
variations: "The customer is always right" used to mean the company eats the loss. Now it means the employee eats the loss. Same sentence, different victim.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Dcard 工作板 「服務業的養奧客及神秘客」 thread; the 「公司花錢請神秘客檢查我，沒花錢請人保護我」 framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
公司每個月花錢請神秘客來檢查我，
卻從來不花錢請人來保護我。
奧客罵我兩小時，主管說：「忍一下。」
神秘客扣我兩分，主管說：「下次注意。」
原來公司的預算表上，
我的尊嚴那一欄是空白的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被神秘客扣分、又被奧客罵到下班的同事。公司願意付錢監督員工，卻不願意付錢保護員工——這不是缺錢，這是選擇。
variations: 公司請得起神秘客來扣分，請不起一句「我們挺員工」。
預算表上有監督員工的錢，沒有保護員工的錢，這就是答案。

en.content:
Every month the company pays a mystery shopper to check on me.
They never pay anyone to protect me.
A rude customer screams at me for two hours — manager says, "Just take it."
A mystery shopper docks me two points — manager says, "Do better."
Turns out on the company's budget sheet,
the line item for my dignity is blank.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who got docked by a mystery shopper and yelled at by a real customer in the same shift. The company can afford to watch you. It can't afford to back you. That's not a budget problem — it's a choice.
variations: The company can pay a mystery shopper to find your mistakes. It just can't pay anyone to say, "We've got your back."
There's a budget line for watching the staff. There isn't one for protecting the staff. That tells you everything.

items.sourceNote: Reframes Buzzfeed 2026 retail-customer round-up; the Macy's-with-a-Target-gift-card anecdote is rewritten as a more universal 跨店通用券 punchline

zh-tw.content:
客人：「我要用這張禮券結帳。」
我：「先生，這是別家的禮券。」
客人：「不都是禮券嗎？」
我：「那我下次去您家，
可以用別人家的鑰匙開門嗎？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近在櫃台被「一張禮券走天下」邏輯轟炸過的同事。鑰匙不通用、信用卡不通用，但客人覺得「禮券」就應該通用——這個邏輯只在櫃台前生效。
variations: 客人覺得禮券是萬國通用，那護照大概也是裝飾品。

en.content:
Customer: "I'd like to pay with this gift card."
Me: "Sir, this is for a different store."
Customer: "It's still a gift card, isn't it?"
Me: "Sure. Next time I come over,
can I open your front door with somebody else's keys?"
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who got hit with the "a gift card is a gift card" logic at the register. Keys aren't universal, credit cards aren't universal — but somehow at the counter, customers think gift cards are. Funny how that logic only fires on the employee's side of the desk.
variations: If gift cards worked at every store, passports would be decoration.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 換日線 「兩岸奧客」 service-industry essay; the 「客人花一杯咖啡的錢，買兩小時的人生顧問」 reframe is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人花一杯咖啡的錢，
要店員聽他講兩小時的人生。
咖啡涼了，他繼續講；
排隊的人多了，他繼續講；
我換班了，他換一個店員繼續講。
他不是來喝咖啡的，
他是來上心理諮商，
而且還想退錢。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被客人當免費諮商師的同事。我們的時薪沒有附「人生顧問」這個項目，但客人總以為一杯拿鐵就買斷了。
variations: 點一杯美式，附贈兩小時人生故事——這個套餐我們真的沒在賣。

en.content:
He pays for one coffee
and expects two hours of life advice.
The coffee goes cold — he keeps talking.
The line gets longer — he keeps talking.
My shift ends — he switches to a different barista and keeps talking.
He didn't come for coffee.
He came for therapy.
And he still wants a refund.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who got turned into a free therapist this week. Our hourly rate doesn't include "life coach" — but customers seem to think a single latte buys it outright.
variations: One Americano plus two free hours of someone's life story — that combo isn't on the menu, and we don't sell it.

items.sourceNote: Reframes TwistedSifter 2026-04 retail-shift-worker story; the 「我給你三個選項你都不要，那你要的選項叫『我說了算』」 framing is original

zh-tw.content:
我給客人 A 方案，他說不要。
我給 B 方案，他說不要。
我給 C 方案，他還是不要。
他要的第四個方案，
叫做「我說了算」。
那個方案不在公司系統裡，
也不在我的薪水裡。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「我都不要、我就是要我要的」客人逼到太陽穴跳的同事。當客人拒絕所有選項，他要的從來不是選項，是權力——而那個權力，公司也沒給你授權。
variations: 客人拒絕所有選項時，他要的不是答案，是聽他指揮的人。
「我都不要」翻譯成中文是：「我要你聽我的」。

en.content:
I offered the customer option A. No.
Option B. No.
Option C. Still no.
The fourth option he actually wanted
is called "because I said so."
That option isn't in the company's system,
and it isn't in my paycheck either.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone whose temples were throbbing today from a customer who rejected every reasonable option. When someone says no to everything, they're not asking for a solution — they're asking for control. And that's a permission your company never gave you to hand out.
variations: When a customer turns down every option, they don't want an answer. They want a subordinate.
"None of those work for me" usually translates to: "I want you to do what I say."

items.sourceNote: Reframes TwistedSifter 2026-04 「客人嫌店員沒微笑」 viral story; rewritten as a 「微笑訓練班」 reverse-coaching joke to avoid copying the original anecdote

zh-tw.content:
客人結完帳，回頭看我說：
「妳要笑一點啦，這樣才有服務業的樣子。」
我笑了。
他說：「妳這個不是真心的笑。」
我說：「先生，您買的是商品，
沒有加購笑容套餐。
要真心笑容，請洽人資，
月薪兩萬六談不到的。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被客人「指導怎麼微笑」的同事。微笑是工作要求，不是免費附贈的情感勞動——而且通常嫌你笑得不夠真的人，才是讓你笑不出來的原因。
variations: 嫌我笑得不夠真的人，剛好就是讓我笑不出來的那個。
想要真心的微笑，請先付出真心的薪水。

en.content:
He paid, turned back, and said:
"You should smile more. That's what service is."
I smiled.
He said: "That smile isn't real."
I said: "Sir, you bought the product.
The sincere-smile add-on isn't included.
For that, please contact HR.
The budget doesn't stretch to it."
en.editorNote: Send this to a coworker who got coached on how to smile by a customer today. Smiling is a job requirement, not a free emotional bonus — and usually the people complaining your smile isn't real are the exact reason it isn't.
variations: The customer who complains my smile isn't real is usually the reason it isn't.
If you want a real smile, fund a real wage first.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 今周刊 「超商萬能店員」 article; the 「結帳、煮咖啡、取包裹、繳水電、清廁所」 stack rewritten as a literal job-title satire

zh-tw.content:
客人問：「你們是便利商店耶，怎麼這個也不會？」
我心裡想：我同時在收銀、煮咖啡、
蒸包子、取包裹、收水電費、
處理 ATM 卡住、清完剛剛那灘咖啡。
我會的事，多到名片印不下，
只是「您要的這一個」剛好不在那張名片上。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給超商同事，特別是被「便利商店怎麼這個也不會」攻擊過的那種早班。我們不是不會，是同時在會七件別的事，輪不到您指定的這一件。
variations: 便利商店店員會的事，多到能寫一張履歷；不會的那一件，剛好就是您要的。
我不是不會，我只是那一秒正在會其他六件事。

en.content:
Customer: "You're a convenience store. How can you not handle this?"
What I'm doing right now: ringing up,
brewing coffee, steaming buns, finding a parcel,
taking a utility bill, unjamming the ATM,
mopping the coffee somebody just spilled.
My skill list is too long for a business card.
The one thing you want just isn't on it.
en.editorNote: Send to a convenience-store coworker — especially anyone who survived the morning shift getting hit with "how can you not handle this." We can handle plenty. We're handling six other things in the second you asked.
variations: A convenience-store clerk's skill list could fill a résumé. The one thing not on it is the one thing you want.
It's not that I can't. It's that I'm currently doing six other things you didn't see.

items.sourceNote: Reframes TwistedSifter 2026-05 「17 歲店員被罵到哭」 story; rewritten as 「『廢物』兩個字的兌換率」 economic metaphor to make a fresh angle

zh-tw.content:
客人罵我「廢物」，
只花了三秒。
我擦掉眼淚回去站櫃台，
花了兩分鐘。
他離開店裡，
那兩個字還在我耳邊響——
他三秒就講完，
我可能要用十年才能忘掉。
這個匯率，也太不公平了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被客人用一句話劃傷的人。我們吞下的不是那句話，是那句話留下的回音——三秒換十年的匯率，全世界沒有任何銀行接受。
variations: 客人三秒鐘就罵完，我們可能要花十年才能忘掉。
他講完那句話就走了，那句話卻在我耳朵裡多住了好幾年。

en.content:
He called me "useless."
It took three seconds.
It took me two minutes to wipe my face and get back to the counter.
He walked out,
but those two words stayed in my ear —
three seconds out of his life,
ten years out of mine.
The exchange rate on this is brutal.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone cut open by a single sentence at work today. What we swallow isn't the sentence — it's the echo. Three seconds traded for ten years, and no bank on earth would honor that rate.
variations: It takes a customer three seconds to insult you. It takes you ten years to unhear it.
He left the store. That sentence rented a room in my head for years.

items.sourceNote: Sparked by Dcard 工作板 「奇葩同事/客人」 trend; rewritten as 「客人請教完店長，回頭問店員一樣的問題」 loop joke (original framing)

zh-tw.content:
客人問我：「我能不能這樣？」
我說：「不行，公司規定。」
他不信，去問店長。
店長說：「不行。」
他不信，去問經理。
經理說：「不行。」
他走回我櫃台，
用很失望的眼神看著我說：
「妳剛剛是不是沒講清楚？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近遇到「同一個答案要從五個人嘴裡聽到才肯信」的客人。我們不是沒講清楚，是您只想聽到「可以」這兩個字——而那兩個字，整層樓都沒有人會說。
variations: 客人問了五個人都說不行，回頭怪第一個人沒講清楚。
客人不是不懂答案，他只是還沒找到願意說「可以」的那個人。

en.content:
Customer: "Can I do this?"
Me: "No, company policy."
He doesn't believe me. Asks the supervisor.
Supervisor: "No."
Doesn't believe him either. Asks the manager.
Manager: "No."
He walks back to my counter,
looks at me with deep disappointment, and says:
"You didn't explain it clearly the first time, did you?"
en.editorNote: Send to anyone whose customer needed the same answer from five different mouths today. We weren't unclear — you were just waiting for somebody to say yes, and nobody on this floor was going to.
variations: Customer asks five people, gets five no's, blames the first person for being unclear.
He's not confused about the answer. He's still hunting for the one person who'll say yes.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Dcard 心情板 「奧客經典語錄」 list — the 「我之前都可以」 vs 「之前的店員不在了」 retort is an original framing

zh-tw.content:
「我之前來都可以欸。」
「請問是哪一位店員幫您處理的？」
「我哪記得。」
「那位同事可能也不記得您了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「我之前都可以」攻擊過的同事。重點是——「之前」這兩個字，是客人手上最萬用的卡，正好我們手上也有一張，叫做「沒這個紀錄」。
variations: 客人的「我之前都可以」，跟前任的「我之前不是這樣的」一樣，都查無實證。
「我之前都可以」翻譯成人話：我希望這次也可以，理由我自己也想不出來。

en.content:
"It worked for me last time."
"Which staff member helped you?"
"How would I remember?"
"They probably don't remember you either."
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who got hit with "but last time it worked" today. "Last time" is the customer's all-purpose wild card — luckily we have one too, and it's called "no record found."
variations: "It worked last time" carries the same evidence as "my ex wasn't like this" — none.
Translation of "it worked last time": I want it to work this time, and I don't have a better reason.

items.sourceNote: Sparked by 2026 Threads viral post about a convenience-store clerk pushed too far; rewritten as 「一句謝謝就抵掉一整天」 reverse angle

zh-tw.content:
今天有個客人離開前
認真地說了一句「謝謝你」。
我整天的疲憊
就被這三個字
結帳完畢、發票一併附上。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給做服務業的朋友，提醒對方：那個讓你想離職的，從來不是工作本身，是不講理的人。願今天有人對你說一句正常的謝謝。
variations: 累的不是這份工作，是那些把「不好意思」當髒話的客人。
服務業的續命包，內容物：一句謝謝、一個微笑、一張正常的臉。

en.content:
A customer left today
and actually said "thank you."
My whole day's exhaustion
got rung up, bagged,
and handed back with the receipt.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone in service work — and remind them: the thing that makes you want to quit isn't the job, it's the unreasonable people. May someone normal thank you today.
variations: It's not the work that's tiring. It's the customers who treat "excuse me" like a swear word.
Service-industry survival kit: one thank-you, one smile, one normal-looking face.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 報導者 2026 service-industry labor-shortage feature; rewritten as 「一個人做三份工」 satire — original framing

zh-tw.content:
我們缺人。
所以一個人要做三個人的工作，
領一個人的薪水，
被三個人份的客人罵。
老闆說這叫「給你成長機會」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「我們是個大家庭」、「現在是團隊一起拚」洗腦的同事。所謂成長，通常是骨頭比薪水先長大。
variations: 公司說「給你成長機會」，翻譯：本來三個人的事，現在你自己想辦法。
缺工的解方不是徵人，是把現有的人榨成三倍——這比較便宜。

en.content:
We're short-staffed.
So one person does three people's work,
gets one person's pay,
and catches three people's worth of complaints.
The boss calls it a "growth opportunity."
en.editorNote: Send to a coworker who's been getting the "we're a family" / "team effort" speech lately. "Growth" usually means your skeleton grows faster than your paycheck.
variations: "Growth opportunity" translated: three people's work, you figure it out.
The solution to a labor shortage isn't hiring — it's squeezing the existing staff three times harder. It's cheaper.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 茶里 《原來服務業是這麼恐怖的》 book themes; the 「微笑是制服的一部分」 metaphor extended into 「員工編號」 angle (original)

zh-tw.content:
客人對我大吼的時候，
我笑著說「不好意思」。
他以為他贏了。
他不知道——
那個笑不是我的，
是員工編號 A2876 的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合下班後配自己的員工證或名牌照。重點是：上班時間那個會微笑的人，跟下班後那個會哭的人，其實是兩個不同的編號。
variations: 他罵的不是我，是我的員工編號。我的本人，下班後才會出現。
服務業的微笑都印在制服上——脫下來那一刻，就會掉一地。

en.content:
When the customer screamed at me,
I smiled and said "I'm sorry."
He thought he won.
He didn't know —
that smile wasn't mine.
It belonged to Employee #A2876.
en.editorNote: Pairs with a post-shift photo of your name tag or employee badge. The point: the person who smiles on shift and the person who cries after clocking out are two different ID numbers.
variations: He wasn't yelling at me. He was yelling at my employee number. The real me clocks in after the shift ends.
Service-industry smiles are printed on the uniform — the moment you take it off, they fall on the floor.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Dcard 2026-04 「奧客會有的經典語錄」 thread; the 「別家都可以」 trope rewritten as a 三段式 dialogue (original phrasing)

zh-tw.content:
「別家都可以，只有你們不行。」
「那您要不要去別家？」
「我就是要在這家。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被「別家都可以」開頭轟炸過的同事。奧客最神奇的地方就是：別家明明都可以，他卻偏偏選你這家。
variations: 說「別家都可以」的客人，從來沒去過別家。
奧客版的忠誠客戶——只忠誠於罵你這一家。

en.content:
"Every other store does it. Only you guys won't."
"Would you like to go to one of those stores?"
"No, I want it from this one."
en.editorNote: Send to a coworker who got hit with the "every other store" opener today. The magic of the difficult customer: every other store can do it, but they still came to yours.
variations: Customers who say "every other store does this" have never actually been to those stores.
The difficult-customer version of brand loyalty — exclusively loyal to yelling at your branch.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Pleated Jeans 2026-05-11 entitled-customer roundup — the 「三步梯太多階」 story; rewritten as a dialogue zinger (original Chinese phrasing)

zh-tw.content:
客人盯著三階的折疊梯看了一個小時，
最後說：「我覺得三階對我來說有點太多階了。」
我沒回他。
我也覺得這一小時對我來說有點太多小時了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合下班後傳給賣場朋友，附一句「你今天又陪客人走過幾座哲學迷宮」。零售業真實事件，連商品名都不用改。
variations: 客人說「三階太多」，店員心想「三句話太多」。
三階的梯子他爬不上去，因為他卡在第一階——『要不要買』。

en.content:
A customer stared at a three-step ladder for an hour,
then said, "I think three steps might be a little too much ladder for me."
I didn't reply.
I thought this hour might be a little too much hour for me.
en.editorNote: Send to a hardware-store friend after their shift with "how many philosophical mazes did you walk a customer through today?" Real retail anecdote — the product name doesn't even need changing.
variations: Customer: "Three steps is too many." Clerk, internally: "Three sentences is too many."
He couldn't climb a three-step ladder because he was stuck on step one: "should I buy it."

items.sourceNote: Inspired by PTT WomenTalk 「服務業的禮貌只是防客訴」 thread; the 防客訴-as-defense-mechanism angle rewritten as a self-aware confession

zh-tw.content:
客人問：「你們服務態度怎麼這麼好？」
我笑：「謝謝。」
心裡想：
這不是好，這是我不想被客訴的本能反應。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合下班後 IG 限動。服務業的禮貌不是修養，是肌肉記憶——被客訴怕了，身體自動會笑。
variations: 我的禮貌不是天生的，是被客訴訓練出來的條件反射。
服務業的「您好」是一句護身符，不是一句問候。

en.content:
Customer: "Wow, your service is so polite."
Me, smiling: "Thank you."
Me, internally:
This isn't politeness. It's the survival reflex of someone who's been complained about before.
en.editorNote: Pairs with a post-shift IG story. Service-industry politeness isn't manners — it's muscle memory. The body smiles automatically once you've survived enough complaints.
variations: My politeness wasn't born. It was trained — by every complaint I ever got.
In service, "How can I help you?" isn't a greeting. It's a charm against the lawsuit gods.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by NotAlwaysRight call-center tag — the 「ghosting a subscription」 punchline rewritten as a Chinese-friendly 「已讀不回退訂」 (original adaptation)

zh-tw.content:
客人：「我已經三個月沒用了，為什麼還在扣款？」
客服：「您要退訂嗎？」
客人：「不用，我以為不用就會自動取消。」
客服心裡：訂閱制不會因為您冷落它就自動失戀。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給客服中心的朋友，配一杯下班後的啤酒。電信、串流、健身房通用——客人以為冷處理可以分手，系統不這麼覺得。
variations: 已讀不回對人有用，對訂閱沒用。
您不滑它，它還是會準時來找您——它叫月費。

en.content:
Customer: "I haven't used it in three months, why am I still being charged?"
Agent: "Would you like to cancel?"
Customer: "No, I assumed it would cancel itself if I stopped using it."
Agent, internally: A subscription doesn't break up with you just because you ignored it.
en.editorNote: Send to a call-center friend with an after-shift beer. Telecom, streaming, gym — the customer thinks the silent treatment is a breakup. The billing system disagrees.
variations: Ghosting works on people. It does not work on subscriptions.
It'll show up on time every month even if you ignore it. We call that one the monthly fee.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 商周 「服務業7年經驗」 column — the 「第一線千萬別待太久」 warning rewritten as a self-reflective monologue (original framing)

zh-tw.content:
前輩說：服務業第一線，千萬別待太久。
我問為什麼。
他說：「你會慢慢忘記，自己也是一個會生氣、會累、會被傷害的人。」
那年我二十五。
今年我終於離職。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合離職那天發一篇 IG 長文，或傳給還在第一線撐著的學弟妹。重點不是叫人逃跑，是提醒——撐到忘記自己也是人之前，要記得回家。
variations: 服務業最危險的不是奧客，是你慢慢覺得「忍下來」是正常的。
離職那一刻，我才想起來：原來我也可以說「我不舒服」。

en.content:
A senior coworker once told me: don't stay on the front line of service too long.
I asked why.
He said, "You start to forget that you're also someone who gets angry, gets tired, gets hurt."
I was twenty-five then.
This year I finally quit.
en.editorNote: Pair with a long IG caption on your last day, or send to a junior who's still grinding the front line. Not a call to run — a reminder to leave before you forget you're a person too.
variations: The real danger in service work isn't difficult customers. It's slowly believing that swallowing it is normal.
The moment I handed in my resignation, I remembered: I'm allowed to say "I'm not okay."

items.sourceNote: Inspired by TwistedSifter 2026-02 viral retail story — customer enters right at closing, lingers 15 mins, then complains the store closed early. Rewritten as a clock-watching monologue (original phrasing)

zh-tw.content:
九點打烊。
八點五十九分客人推門進來，
九點十五分還在挑第三件商品。
結帳時他說：「你們今天怎麼這麼早關？」
我看著手錶，
手錶看著我，
我們兩個都不敢說話。
zh-tw.editorNote: 獻給所有曾在打烊前一分鐘看見門開的零售人。傳給輪到關店班的朋友，附一句「你今天的門能順利鎖上嗎」。
variations: 客人說「我只看一下」，這句話的計量單位通常是「半小時」。
服務業的時間有兩種：客人的時間、和員工想下班的時間，這兩者永遠對不上。

en.content:
We close at nine.
At 8:59, the door opens.
At 9:15, he's still picking item number three.
At checkout he says, "Why are you closing so early today?"
I look at my watch.
My watch looks at me.
Neither of us says anything.
en.editorNote: For everyone who's seen that door swing open one minute before lockup. Send to a friend on closing shift with "think you'll get to lock the door tonight?"
variations: When a customer says "I'll just look real quick," the standard unit of measurement is half an hour.
Service-industry time runs on two clocks: the customer's, and the staff's countdown to clock out. They never line up.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 商周 服務業7年經驗 column — extends the 「薪水與勞力不對等」 thread into a fresh paycheck-day monologue (original framing)

zh-tw.content:
發薪日當天，
我在櫃檯被客人罵了十五分鐘。
下班看薪資單，
我算了一下，
那十五分鐘大概值新台幣三十二塊。
我給自己買了一杯超商咖啡，
找零還剩兩塊——剛好夠買回我的尊嚴的一個音節。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合月底發薪日當天放限動。重點不是抱怨低薪，是把「被罵」換算成時薪，那種冷冷的荒謬感。
variations: 薪水不是不夠，是被客人罵的那十五分鐘扣得太準。
服務業的時薪要分兩種算：站著的、和站著被罵的。

en.content:
Payday.
I got chewed out at the counter for fifteen minutes.
After the shift I looked at the pay slip,
did the math,
and those fifteen minutes were worth about a dollar twenty.
I bought myself a convenience-store coffee.
Got eight cents back —
just enough to buy back one syllable of my dignity.
en.editorNote: Drop on your IG story on payday. Not a paycheck complaint — a cold little arithmetic where "getting yelled at" becomes an hourly rate.
variations: The pay isn't too low. The fifteen minutes of getting yelled at are just priced too accurately.
Service-industry wages come in two rates: standing, and standing while being yelled at.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by NotAlwaysRight retail tag — the 「I-want-to-speak-to-the-manager」 trope rewritten as a self-aware Chinese punchline (original adaptation)

zh-tw.content:
「我要找你們店長。」
「我就是店長。」
「那我要找你們老闆。」
「我就是老闆。」
「⋯⋯那我要找你們總公司。」
「先生，這是一人公司。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給自己當老闆兼員工的小店朋友，附一句「今天有人想找你的總公司嗎」。獨立小店主每月至少被這樣問過一次。
variations: 找店長、找老闆、找總公司——客人不是要解決問題，是想找一個比你更怕他的人。
一人公司的好處：客訴永遠在三秒內傳達到管理層。

en.content:
"I want to speak to your manager."
"I am the manager."
"Then I want to speak to the owner."
"I am the owner."
"...Then I want to speak to corporate."
"Sir, this is a one-person company."
en.editorNote: Send to a friend running a solo shop with "anyone ask for your corporate office today?" Indie owners get this one at least once a month.
variations: Asking for the manager, the owner, then corporate — the customer isn't trying to solve anything. They're hunting for someone more scared of them than you are.
Upside of a one-person company: every complaint reaches upper management within three seconds.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by PressLogic 12句職場厭世金句 column — extends the 「微笑變肌肉痠痛」 motif into a fresh facial-fatigue confession (original phrasing)

zh-tw.content:
下班回家照鏡子，
發現我的笑肌還沒下班。
它已經習慣性翹起來了，
就算我已經沒有想笑的理由。
身體比心更敬業，
這大概是服務業最殘忍的職業病。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合下班洗完臉後對著鏡子拍一張，配這段文字放限動。重點不是裝可憐，是承認笑容也會變成一種肌肉記憶。
variations: 服務業的職業病不是腰痠，是臉痠。
下班的我還在笑，那不是開心，是肌肉忘了關機。

en.content:
I got home, looked in the mirror,
and realized my smile muscles hadn't clocked out yet.
The corners of my mouth were still up,
even though I had nothing left to smile about.
The body stays on duty longer than the heart.
That's probably the cruelest occupational hazard in service work.
en.editorNote: Pairs with a post-shift mirror selfie on your IG story. Not a pity post — just admitting that a smile can become muscle memory.
variations: The occupational injury of service work isn't a sore back. It's a sore face.
I'm off the clock and still smiling. That's not happiness. That's a muscle that forgot to power down.
